metadata.title: Build an STM Tunneling Curve with Claude Code
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = tunneling curve. CRIMSON = 7x marker points. INK for all Text.

then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'MedhavyOpen' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B05: palette=claude but the outro is 'MedhavyOutro' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Medhavy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

lines: — the perfect name for our AI-powered

beats.narration_text: The prompt. Claude Code terminal: claude — plot tunneling current versus tip-to-sample distance for an S-T-M. Work function phi equals four electron-volts. Use the W-K-B formula: current proportional to e to the minus two kappa d. kappa equals root two m phi over h-bar. Show d from one to ten angstroms on a semi-log axis. Mark the point where d increases by one angstrom and the current drops by seven-point-four. Specify: the exponential decay, the slope, and the seven-times rule.

props.command:
claude "plot STM tunneling current vs gap d:
  φ=4 eV, κ=√(2mφ)/ℏ ≈ 1.02 Å⁻¹
  I ∝ exp(−2κd)   [WKB tunneling]
  semi-log axis: d ∈ [1,10] Å
  mark: +1 Å → I drops by factor e²≈7.4"

beats.narration_text: The CLI writes it. Read before running. Two checks. First: the kappa value. The formula is root of two m phi over h-bar. For phi equals four electron-volts, kappa must come out near one-point-zero-two inverse angstroms. If it reads one-point-four-four, the code used the wrong unit conversion. Second: the slope. On a natural-log plot, the slope should equal minus two kappa — approximately minus two-point-zero-four per angstrom. Thirty seconds. Catch it before the render.

props.code:
# stm.py  —  Claude Code output
import numpy as np

# WKB tunneling: I ∝ exp(−2κd)
hbar_eVs = 6.582e-16   # eV·s
m_eV    = 0.511e6      # eV/c²
phi_eV  = 4.0          # work function (eV)
c_Angps = 2.998e10     # Å/s (speed of light)

kappa = np.sqrt(2 * m_eV * phi_eV) / (hbar_eVs * c_Angps)
# kappa ≈ 1.024 Å⁻¹  →  2κ ≈ 2.048 Å⁻¹
# +1 Å → current × exp(−2.048) ≈ 1/7.7

beats.narration_text: Run it. Two checks. First: the slope. On the semi-log plot the teal curve is a straight line. Its slope reads minus two kappa — approximately minus two-point-zero-four inverse angstroms. Every extra angstrom of gap multiplies the log-current by that slope. Second: the seven-point-four markers. The two crimson dots — one angstrom apart — show a current ratio of e-squared, approximately seven-point-four. That is Binnig and Rohrer's rule: pull the tip back by one angstrom and the current falls by a factor of seven. The periodic table of surface atoms is built on that sensitivity.

Iterate: claude — update stm dot py — add a second curve for phi equals five electron-volts. Steeper slope, faster drop. One diff, one re-run. Prompt, read, run, check, change. The tunneling curve was the vehicle. The loop is the takeaway.

props.command:
claude "update stm.py:
  → add curve for φ=5 eV (steeper slope)
  → label both curves"

beats.narration_text: Medhavy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at Medhavy A-I. Medhavy dot com.
